metadata.summary: Technical and analytical groundwork for valid A/B experiments: accurate sample-size calculation using statsmodels, traffic-split configuration with deterministic-hash bucketing, SRM monitoring from day 1, and pre-launch and results documentation templates.

content.title: A/B Testing Setup
content.summary: A/B testing setup covers the technical and analytical groundwork for a valid experiment: accurate sample-size calculation (statsmodels NormalIndPower, not the simplified 16×p(1-p)/MDE² teaching formula), traffic-split configuration with deterministic-hash bucketing, SRM monitoring from day 1, and a plan-and-results template that an analyst agent fills in deterministically.

why.p: The single biggest experiment-program failure mode is not low K-factor or poor hypothesis quality — it is underpowered tests that produce noise mistaken for signal. Using statsmodels gives honest sample-size math; the simplified formula under- or over-estimates by 25–50% for non-50/50 splits, one-sided tests, or asymmetric baselines. Pre-committing to end dates and wiring SRM day 1 prevents the two most common invalidation patterns.

list.item: Pre-launch sample-size and duration calculation for any planned experiment.
Configuring traffic split, randomization, and SRM monitoring in Statsig, GrowthBook, LaunchDarkly, or equivalent.
Auditing a draft test plan against the pre-launch checklist before traffic flows.
Generating plan and results templates that analysts fill in consistently across the team.
Network-effect surfaces (chat, marketplace, multiplayer) — independent randomization is invalid; use cluster or switchback designs.
Heavy personalization where every user already sees a unique variant — plain A/B math doesn't apply.
Single-shot critical decisions (pricing model, brand direction) — blast radius too large.
Pre-instrumentation: if the primary metric is not tracked end-to-end yet, instrument first; computing sample size is meaningless otherwise.
Continuous heavy-tailed outcome metrics (revenue per user) without log-transform or bootstrap — proportion math misleads.

section.name: sample-size-calculation-rules
section.p: Accurate sample-size and duration calculation using statsmodels, with common pitfalls of the simplified formula and a quick reference table by baseline rate and MDE.

rule.statement: Use statsmodels NormalIndPower for all sample-size calculations; the simplified n = 16*p*(1-p)/MDE^2 is a teaching aid — it silently assumes 95% two-sided, 80% power, and 50/50 split.
rule.rationale: Deviations from those defaults (one-sided test, 90/10 split, 90% power) cause 25–50% error in the simplified formula. statsmodels handles all cases correctly.

rule.statement: Always state MDE as relative percentage (e.g. 10% relative lift on a 5% baseline = 0.5pp absolute), and convert explicitly in the calculation output.
rule.rationale: MDE relative vs absolute confusion is the single biggest sample-size error — an agent told "MDE = 1%" on a 5% baseline can compute for a 1pp absolute lift (doubling the metric) rather than a 10% relative lift.

example.note: baseline=10%, MDE="10%" — ambiguous: does this mean +1pp (absolute) or +10% relative (to 11%)?

rule.statement: Account for weekend traffic dips when estimating duration; if daily_users is based on a Monday-Friday average, multiply by 7/5 to get true daily average before dividing into total sample.
rule.rationale: Agents using weekday-only traffic estimate durations that are 30–50% too short; the test hits its end date before reaching the required sample.

section.p: Quick reference: n_per_variant at alpha=0.05, power=0.80, two-sided

rule.statement: When using a 90/10 split to limit blast radius, pass ratio=9.0 to NormalIndPower; the small arm needs ~9x more users per-arm than a 50/50 split to achieve the same power.
rule.rationale: Asymmetric splits blow up sample requirement on the small arm; agents that forget the ratio parameter under-size and produce unreliable results.

section.reference: Python function: n_per_variant and duration_days using NormalIndPower + proportion_effectsize

section.name: randomization-and-monitoring
section.p: Randomization rules, SRM monitoring setup, A/A test, sequential testing opt-in, and holdout bucket design for valid experiment infrastructure.

rule.statement: Use deterministic-hash bucketing (hash(user_id + experiment_id) % 100) so users stay in the same arm across sessions and devices.
rule.rationale: Session-based random assignment causes the same user to see both variants across sessions, contaminating results.

rule.statement: Reserve a 5% holdout bucket outside all experiments for clean baseline measurement.
rule.rationale: Without a holdout, long-running experiment programs cannot measure the aggregate effect of the full test calendar on the product metrics.

rule.statement: Configure an SRM check (chi-square on observed split vs expected) on day 1 and alert when p < 0.001; invalidate the test if the alert fires and investigate before resuming.
rule.rationale: SRM indicates that randomization is broken — the test result cannot be trusted even if the primary metric reaches significance.

list.item: expected 50/50 split → observed n_A vs n_B → chi2 p-value
if p < 0.001 → pause, investigate tracking and bucketing before proceeding
common SRM causes: bot traffic, caching bypasses partial variant, SDK version mismatch

rule.statement: Run an A/A test (both arms see control) for 1–2 days before the real experiment; confirm no false positive on the primary metric before introducing the treatment.
rule.rationale: A/A tests catch tracking bugs, bucketing errors, and pre-existing metric imbalances that would invalidate results even before treatment is introduced.

rule.statement: If the experiment platform supports sequential testing (Statsig, Optimizely), opt in — it allows valid peeking at any time without inflating false positives.
rule.rationale: Fixed-horizon tests require committing to an end date and not peeking; sequential designs are more flexible but require the platform to use a valid always-valid inference method.

antipattern.description: Using fixed-horizon sample-size math and then analyzing with sequential testing tools.
antipattern.reason: The two frameworks have different stopping rules; mixing them produces invalid confidence intervals.

section.name: pre-launch-checklist
section.p: Full setup checklist covering technical platform, analytics configuration, team training, success-metrics definition, and experiment governance.

list.item: install experiment platform (Statsig / GrowthBook / LaunchDarkly / PostHog)
configure deterministic-hash bucketing with user_id + experiment_id
set up SRM monitoring with day-1 chi-square alert
wire analytics integration (GA4, Mixpanel, Amplitude, or warehouse)
QA tracking in staging before first live test
define primary metric SQL in a versioned model
add custom events for all secondary and guardrail metrics
create user segment for test participants (exclude bots, staff)
verify conversion tracking fires on correct events in both variants
confirm no partial-day contamination (ramp-up traffic excluded from analysis window)
train team on if/then/because hypothesis format
document MDE relative vs absolute distinction and calculator link
create experiment log template (hypothesis, result, lift, learning)
establish weekly test-launch meeting and sign-off procedure
define governance rule: no test launches without analyst approval on sample size
choose primary metric — must tie to a business outcome, not a vanity metric
define 3+ guardrail metrics explicitly as "watch only"
set baseline value from the last 28 days
calculate MDE based on minimum business-relevant improvement
compute n_per_variant and duration using statsmodels (not simplified formula)
set confidence level (standard: alpha=0.05, power=0.80)

section.name: agent-integration
section.p: Agentic workflow and common failure modes.

An `opus` design agent runs the pre-launch checklist (hypothesis, primary, secondaries, guardrails, MDE) and computes sample size + duration with explicit alpha/power/split. A `sonnet` config agent translates the plan into the experiment platform's API call (Statsig/GrowthBook/LaunchDarkly). A `haiku` monitor watches SRM and traffic-allocation health daily. The same `opus` agent reads results at the pre-committed end date and writes the learning into the experiment log.

list.item: Agent reports "duration: 7 days" without checking whether traffic includes a weekend; weekend traffic dips by 30-50%.
The simplified formula uses two-sided alpha=0.05 implicitly; agents on one-sided tests under-size by ~25%.
Asymmetric splits (90/10 to limit blast radius) blow up the sample requirement on the small arm; agent must apply the ratio correctly.
SRM check is often skipped in templates — wire it into the pre-launch checklist explicitly.
Sample-size answers in "users" can confuse a per-session metric — agent should declare the unit of randomization.
Experiment-platform API agents that auto-create experiments without the analytical-plan attachment break audit trails.
Prompt agents to refuse the test if MDE is missing rather than picking a default; a "default 10%" silently misleads.

template.purpose: Python: accurate n_per_variant and duration_days using statsmodels
